b' What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing: A New Oscars Plan, Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x98Wuthering\xe2\x80\x99 Bid & Bela\xe2\x80\x99s Book
Welcome back to What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing, and thanks to those who came to my chat this morning with Lucas Shaw at the Bloomberg Screentime conference. Our live recording of The Town on October 24 at NeueHouse Hollywood is sold out, but there will be another one as part of Puck\xe2\x80\x99s Stories of the Season awards event on November 15. Request an invite by emailing Fritz@puck.news. \xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c

Welcome back to What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing, and thanks to those who came to my chat this morning with Lucas Shaw at the Bloomberg Screentime conference. Some personal highlights from the two-day event:

  • Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s Brian Roberts appearing to almost say that David Zaslav is doing a good job at Warner Discovery before checking himself and instead praising Zaslav for \xe2\x80\x9cworking really hard.\xe2\x80\x9d

  • Live Nation\xe2\x80\x99s Michael Rapino saying, \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re a 2 percent-margin business, so we must be the dumbest monopoly alive,\xe2\x80\x9d despite the fact that one very Google-able possible indication of a monopoly is when a business is so large and dominant that it can thrive on small margins that would prevent others from competing.

  • Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s Bela Bajaria saying that the company is not interested in changing its compensation structure while acknowledging that it has changed its compensation structure in a few recent instances (see below).

  • Producer Jason Blum attempting to have a serious business conversation about the impact of the labor strikes while stuffing his face with chicken wings on Hot Ones .

  • Analyst Rich Greenfield \xe2\x80\x99s lilac dress shirt.
Our live recording of The Town on October 24 at NeueHouse Hollywood is sold out, but there will be another one as part of Puck\xe2\x80\x99s Stories of the Season awards event on November 15. Request an invite by emailing Fritz@puck.news.

Also, a big congrats to Puck\xe2\x80\x99s fashion expert Lauren Sherman , whose new book about the rise and fall of Victoria\xe2\x80\x99s Secret, Selling Sexy , scored a great review in the Times . Order it here . (Yes, Michael Bay \xe2\x80\x99s in it, of course.)

Got a news tip or an idea for me? Just reply to this email or message me anonymously on Signal at 310-804-3198.

Let\xe2\x80\x99s begin\xe2\x80\xa6
A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR
Ralph Fiennes , Stanley Tucci , John Lithgow and Isabella Rossellini star in this gripping thriller that follows one of the world\xe2\x80\x99s most secretive and ancient events - selecting the new Pope. What happens behind these walls will change everything.

From Academy Award-winning director Edward Berger
CONCLAVE
Only in theaters October 25

WATCH THE TRAILER & GET TICKETS
Thursday Thoughts\xe2\x80\xa6
  • Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s no-theaters issue strikes again : Dan Lin , the new head of Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s film unit, is quickly becoming familiar with the downside of refusing to put movies in theaters. According to three sources, Lin has bid more than $150 million for Saltburn filmmaker Emerald Fennell \xe2\x80\x99s planned adaptation of Wuthering Heights , with Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi set to star. That\xe2\x80\x99s far more than other bidders, I\xe2\x80\x99m told, and MRC, the film finance company that put the package together, would very much like to take the money. But Fennell and Robbie, who\xe2\x80\x99s also producing, fear the movie won\xe2\x80\x99t have the same cultural impact without a theatrical release and marketing campaign that the rivals are offering. And, let\xe2\x80\x99s be honest, they\xe2\x80\x99re right . So they\xe2\x80\x99re wavering, and as of today, Netflix isn\xe2\x80\x99t budging on theaters.

    \xe2\x80\x9cNot our model,\xe2\x80\x9d Netflix chief content officer Bela Bajaria likes to say, echoing her boss, co-C.E.O. Ted Sarandos . Bajaria repeated that line today at the Screentime conference, adding, \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re just in a different business.\xe2\x80\x9d That\xe2\x80\x99s true\xe2\x80\xa6 and also kinda not true. Netflix is ultimately in the talent business, just like all its rivals, and those that offer the best talent a theatrical release are still in a better position to win the best film projects\xe2\x80\x94unless there\xe2\x80\x99s a willingness to vastly overpay. What\xe2\x80\x99s been true since the advent of the TV movie in the 1960s is still true today.

    Similar concerns are playing out with Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s big Chronicles of Narnia movie, which Barbie director Greta Gerwig has been signed on to write and direct since back in 2020. Gerwig has also been raising concerns with Lin about the theater issue, given the scope and grandeur of the material. It\xe2\x80\x99s all been friendly requests so far\xe2\x80\x94demands and screaming isn\xe2\x80\x99t really Gerwig\xe2\x80\x99s style\xe2\x80\x94but she\xe2\x80\x99s clearly bummed about the platform she\xe2\x80\x99s being offered\xe2\x80\x94and not offered. Netflix, which has the rights to all the Narnia books, would very much like to give an A-list director what she wants. But, again, theaters are not their model .

    Back to Wuthering Heights . I\xe2\x80\x99m told this one will ultimately be Fennell\xe2\x80\x99s decision. But Robbie is one of the savviest actor-producers in town, and it\xe2\x80\x99s probably not a coincidence that she\xe2\x80\x99s also one of the few stars that has never done a Netflix movie. Without making a Knives Out 2 -style exception for theaters, Dan Lin is really gonna need to pay up.

  • More Bela : Let\xe2\x80\x99s all bookmark this comment from Bajaria today: \xe2\x80\x9cWe are not changing our compensation model.\xe2\x80\x9d Hmmm . Given Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s experimentation in shifting a few film buyouts to backends, rival Apple TV+\xe2\x80\x99s interest in doing the same, and the many, many internal conversations about \xe2\x80\x9cevolving\xe2\x80\x9d its pay structure, what\xe2\x80\x99s the over/under on how long this public position lasts? As was the case with the pivots from longtime Netflix no-nos like advertising, sports, and password-sharing, I\xe2\x80\x99m guessing the company will soon \xe2\x80\x9cdiscover\xe2\x80\x9d that the only way to manage the cost of content is to pay premiums to some talent only in success. I\xe2\x80\x99m looking forward to going on this journey with Bajaria.

  • Bela, the book : Speaking of Bajaria, congrats are in order. I\xe2\x80\x99m told she just sold a personal memoir to Little Brown/Hachette. Logline: \xe2\x80\x9cThe book explores Bajaria's path to success, braiding lessons from the Indian upbringing she rebelled against as a young person in her unique pursuit of the American Dream.\xe2\x80\x9d A Netflix rep confirmed Bajaria is writing the book but declined to comment. No publishing date is set.

  • \xe2\x80\x98Wicked\xe2\x80\x99 vs. \xe2\x80\x98Gladiator,\xe2\x80\x99 the upcharge war : Contracts are now signed, and Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s Gladiator II has emerged with the lion\xe2\x80\x99s share of Imax screens worldwide for the November 22 weekend. This isn\xe2\x80\x99t an Oppenheimer vs. Mission: Impossible\xe2\x80\x94Dead Reckoning situation, where Chris Nolan filmed sequences in Imax and thus got a commitment that bigfooted Tom Cruise last summer. The Imax people simply believe Gladiator will index better with their male-skewing fan base. Universal, countering the move, has secured more of the non-Imax large-format screens for Wicked .

  • Why even bribe Chris Nolan? : Sure felt like Universal was twisting the knife on Warner Bros. by finally confirming the next Chris Nolan movie the day after those atrocious Joker 2 box office numbers came in. WB co-chief Mike De Luca had been pretty openly campaigning to return Nolan to his \xe2\x80\x9chome\xe2\x80\x9d studio after the falling-out over the previous regime\xe2\x80\x99s day-and-date strategy for HBO Max. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re hoping to get Nolan back,\xe2\x80\x9d De Luca told Variety last summer. \xe2\x80\x9cI think there\xe2\x80\x99s a world.\xe2\x80\x9d De Luca even convinced his boss, David Zaslav , to cut Nolan a seven-figure \xe2\x80\x9croyalty\xe2\x80\x9d check for Tenet as a make-good to fix the relationship. Unfortunately for Warners, Oppenheimer grossed nearly $1 billion and won seven Oscars. And predictably, neither De Luca nor his co-chief, Pam Abdy , was allowed to even read the script for Nolan\xe2\x80\x99s next movie before Universal\xe2\x80\x99s Donna Langley snapped it up.

  • Box office over/under: Speaking of Joker\xe2\x80\xa6 Terrifier 3 , with its tiny production budget, could actually beat the second weekend of the nearly $200 million Folie \xc3\xa0 Deux . So for comedy\xe2\x80\x99s sake, I\xe2\x80\x99ll take the over on the $11 million tracking.
The Oscars\xe2\x80\x99 No-Big-Deal Rebrand
What if hosting Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s marquee awards show were less of a heavy lift, with the added benefit of a telecast with more stars and performers who went beyond simply presenting. That\xe2\x80\x99s what the Academy wants. Could it work?
MATTHEW BELLONI
This one\xe2\x80\x99s amusing: The film Academy believes it has come up with a solution to its annual problem of finding someone meaningful who\xe2\x80\x99s willing to host the damn Oscars. In recent communications, both internally and to the talent community, Academy executives all the way up to C.E.O. Bill Kramer and president Janet Yang are talking about a \xe2\x80\x9clighter lift\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94a scenario where a host wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be required to do a 12-minute monologue, or produce multiple bits and taped pieces, or rehearse lavish musical numbers, or\xe2\x80\xa6 you get it.

The idea, I\xe2\x80\x99m told, is to make the host gig easier to handle and, thus, more appealing. In an era when hosting the Oscars comes with less upside from the declining audience and more downsides, like the massive time suck and getting eviscerated on social media\xe2\x80\x94for almost no pay, don\xe2\x80\x99t forget\xe2\x80\x94it\xe2\x80\x99s no wonder the job is no longer attractive. These days, when top actors and comics are working all over the world and booked back-to-back, often a year or two in advance, dropping it all to spend a couple months preparing for a high-profile night of television isn\xe2\x80\x99t a priority.

Hence, no host is locked in for March\xe2\x80\x99s show after Jimmy Kimmel declined to return and John Mulaney passed on the opportunity. To all but a thin sliver of performers (most of whom wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t serve as effective promoters and thus wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t appeal to the Academy or ABC) or the most dutiful of company men (like Kimmel, who\xe2\x80\x99s hosted four very funny times\xe2\x80\xa6 or almost every Emmy host), it\xe2\x80\x99s a polite pass. And I don\xe2\x80\x99t blame them.

But\xe2\x80\xa6 what if the Oscars were essentially rebranded to the talent community as less of a big deal? A host could expect to spend fewer weeks preparing, and would be supported by other changes to the show. As part of this plan, I\xe2\x80\x99m told, the Academy would prioritize stacking the telecast with more stars and performers who go beyond simply presenting\xe2\x80\x94stuff like Mulaney\xe2\x80\x99s great Field of Dreams routine from March\xe2\x80\x99s show, or the extended Tina Fey - Amy Poehler - Maya Rudolph moment at the hostless 2019 show. Or more stunts, live performances, ambitious tributes, and incorporating music (like Slash and Mark Ronson this year) and social media stars and exclusive trailers into what essentially becomes a Comic-Con-style extravaganza of promotion for movies. In these scenarios, the host would essentially be a circus ring-leader rather than a headlining act.
\xe2\x80\x9cLet\xe2\x80\x99s Celebrate\xe2\x80\x9d
That\xe2\x80\x99s the idea, at least. I know, the Academy tends to say a lot and do very little, and it will all depend on talent buy-in. I\xe2\x80\x99ve heard there\xe2\x80\x99s already been some outreach to stars who have had a big year. Particularly stars in the orbit of Disney, which is most invested in the ABC show. If Disney ends up having the top three movies of 2024 ( Inside Out 2 , Deadpool & Wolverine , and likely Moana 2 ), why not try to plumb Poehler, Ryan Reynolds and Hugh Jackman , and Dwayne Johnson to either host in a reduced capacity or contribute to the show in some way? They\xe2\x80\x99ve talked to Will Ferrell , whose new documentary, Will & Harper , has a chance to get nominated. The Wicked ladies are on the list, nominated or not. The pitch now is to either host the show or sign up for a piece of the show, like bringing a hot dish to Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s version of a big potluck dinner.

The plan does not include eliminating any of the 23 categories from the live telecast, which I\xe2\x80\x99ve long argued is the only way to truly reinvent the show for a modern audience. When I asked the Academy to elaborate on what I was hearing about the potentially reduced role of the host, a rep declined to confirm the details. Instead, Kramer sent me a statement confirming some of the other plans, saying he wants to \xe2\x80\x9ccontinue to evolve the Oscars to be nimble and dynamic.\xe2\x80\x9d

So\xe2\x80\xa6 what does that mean? \xe2\x80\x9cWe want the Oscars to be the place where studios and filmmakers unveil the most exciting things about their work\xe2\x80\x94beloved characters and stories, legacy moments, and upcoming releases,\xe2\x80\x9d Kramer said. \xe2\x80\x9cNo other venue brings together a global community of film fans like the Oscars. Let\xe2\x80\x99s celebrate our love of the movies and our industry together."

That\xe2\x80\x99s easier said than done. I\xe2\x80\x99ve advocated turning the Oscars into a night of exclusive trailers and footage of upcoming blockbusters. But that requires studio support, and getting big stars to show up as part of their promotional duties for movies that might not open for months. It\xe2\x80\x99s currently not cool to go to the Oscars unless you are nominated. Heck, the Academy couldn\xe2\x80\x99t even get Tom Cruise and James Cameron to show up to the 2023 show when they were nominated. Everyone still wants to win an Oscar, of course, but walk the ravenous red carpet gauntlet and sit through the 3.5-hour show when a gold statue isn\xe2\x80\x99t even a possibility? Most who matter would rather just go straight to Beyonc\xc3\xa9 and Jay-Z \xe2\x80\x99s party.

So I\xe2\x80\x99m skeptical. But at least the Academy is acknowledging it needs to make changes. Talks for a new television/streaming deal are about to begin, and it\xe2\x80\x99s gonna take some momentum to get anywhere near the $90 million a year that Disney/ABC currently pays. It\xe2\x80\x99s a big problem.

Back in 2021 , a few months after the infamous train station Oscars, I wrote, \xe2\x80\x9cAwards shows, in general, and the Oscars, in particular, seem increasingly like an outdated television format, destined to take its place in obscurity with soaps and Westerns.\xe2\x80\x9d But since then, there\xe2\x80\x99s actually been snippets of encouraging news. The most recent Oscars inched up 4 percent in viewership, to 19.5 million viewers, thanks mostly to Barbenheimer, I think. (Disney, unbelievably, still does not air the show live on Hulu, thanks to its linear affiliate agreements.) The Golden Globes got themselves back on Sunday night television on CBS. Netflix (SAG Awards) and Prime Video (Academy of Country Music Awards) have experimented with the format. Even the lowest-tier show, the Critics Choice Awards, found a new deal on E! and Peacock. It\xe2\x80\x99s not all good news. The American Music Awards\xe2\x80\x94which hilariously celebrated its 50th anniversary on Sunday with a sad tribute show despite having already celebrated its 50th anniversary a few years ago\xe2\x80\x94cratered in the ratings on CBS, an add-on in the recent Globes deal.

Of course, the Academy\xe2\x80\x99s plans could all change if a big name decides he or she wants to host the old-fashioned way. But if not, maybe this is a decent strategy to improve the show. If lessening the host workload helps in any way to get more star power onto what is still Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s biggest platform to promote itself to the world, it\xe2\x80\x99s probably worth it.
See you Monday,
Matt

Got a question, comment, complaint, or want to host the Oscars? Email me at Matt@puck.news or call/text me at 310-804-3198.
